summary: Stancil argues that SQL’s dominance has encouraged data teams to model business logic directly in queries, but many real business processes aren’t relational and get poorly approximated as a result. He suggests that semantic layers should move beyond pure SQL, via richer logic languages (like Jinja-heavy dbt or Malloy) or even non-SQL databases, to capture complex business models more faithfully.
money_quotes: Perhaps, to nail a disputation on the church door, one of the core responsibilities of a data team—modeling a business, and defining a semantic layer—is best done in another language.
This type of semantic layer has its problems, though. Most notably, there is notrue model.
In other words, defining a semantic layer in pure SQL is like animating a movie by hand.

Which leads us to the heretical conclusion: Just as SQL can, but shouldn’t, be used to define complex statistical analysis, perhaps SQL can, but probably shouldn’t, be used to model the complex operations of a business.
Every once in a while, though, that which is supposed to be certain, isn't.
key_insight: SQL-centric semantic layers make business modeling accessible but brittle, and richer logic-driven layers may be necessary to represent complex operations accurately.
